summary.detailed_summary: EvoBrain proposes a task-aware continual learning framework to replace static, task-isolated fine-tuning for EEG foundation models. It introduces Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization (NSN) to align temporal statistics and recalibrate spectral responses, alongside Response-Affinity Distillation (RAD) to preserve old-task response geometry and enable selective knowledge transfer. Evaluations across six diverse BCI tasks using four different foundation backbones demonstrate that the model effectively balances plasticity and stability, retaining over 91% of single-task performance.

headline_results: Retained 91.3% of single-task fine-tuning performance
Relative Forgetting Rate (RFR) below 4%

key_points: Method for post-training EEG foundation models: EvoBrain enables a single unified backbone to incrementally learn diverse BCI tasks via a continual learning paradigm.
Combines Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization (NSN) for distributional alignment and Response-Affinity Distillation (RAD) to mitigate catastrophic forgetting.
Demonstrates architecture-agnostic efficacy across Transformer and Mamba backbones, maintaining low forgetting rates across six heterogeneous EEG datasets.
limitations: Assumes known task boundaries and task-specific heads.
Relies on replay memory, which may raise privacy concerns in clinical settings.
Lacks subject-level validation for the spectral modulation patterns.
Does not address online settings with ambiguous task identities.

method.architecture: Architecture-agnostic framework integrating Neuro-Spectral Task Normalization and Response-Affinity Distillation.
method.finetuning: Continual learning via task-aware normalization, response-geometry distillation, and time-dependent replay.
method.objective: Supervised classification with distillation loss to preserve previous task knowledge.
method.pretraining: Utilizes existing EEG-FMs (CBraMod, EEGMamba, CSBrain, DeeperBrain) as backbones.

summary.one_liner: A continual learning framework for EEG foundation models that enables a single backbone to evolve across heterogeneous BCI tasks without catastrophic forgetting.
summary.title: EvoBrain: Continual Learning of EEG Foundation Models Across Heterogeneous BCI Tasks
summary.unique_contribution: Pioneers cross-task continual learning for EEG foundation models using a combination of neuro-spectral normalization and affinity-aware response distillation.

papers.title: EvoBrain: Continual Learning of EEG Foundation Models Across Heterogeneous BCI Tasks

reasons: Explicitly targets the post-training and downstream adaptation of EEG foundation models.
Proposes a continual learning framework (EvoBrain) specifically for unified EEG decoding using foundation model backbones.
Fits the ecosystem criteria for adaptation, fine-tuning, and scaling analysis of EEG-FMs.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper introduces FMScope, a frozen-representation diagnostic protocol consisting of five tools to audit EEG foundation models for subject-identity confounding. It reveals a universal 'Identity Trap' where subject variance dominates representations and is amplified during fine-tuning, regardless of the clinical label's presence. Evidence from three FMs across four clinical datasets shows that removing the subject axis—often carried by the aperiodic 1/f background—can improve label decoding when a genuine cross-subject marker exists.

headline_results: Subject-variance fraction is 13-89x a random null
Erasing subject axis improves label decoding by +6 to +12 percentage points in primary cells
tasks: Clinical resting-state EEG classification (mental arithmetic, Alzheimer's/FTD, sleep deprivation, trait stress)

key_points: Benchmark diagnostic framework: FMScope audits frozen EEG-FM representations to distinguish genuine clinical biomarkers from subject-identity shortcuts.
Discovery of the 'Identity Trap': Subject variance dominates representations across tested FMs and is amplified during fine-tuning, often acting as a shortcut.
Physiological carrier: For LaBraM and CBraMod, the aperiodic 1/f background is a primary carrier of subject identity, which can be linearly erased to improve label decoding.
limitations: Relies on one dataset per cell in the 2x2 layout
Small sample sizes (N=14 to 65 subjects per dataset)
Aperiodic ablation is a correlational intervention, not necessarily causal
Single-session ADFTD cannot separate disease state from stable traits by data alone

method.finetuning: Linear classification head, full fine-tuning vs. frozen linear probe

summary.one_liner: A diagnostic audit revealing that EEG foundation models often rely on subject-identity shortcuts (the 'Identity Trap') rather than genuine clinical biomarkers.
summary.title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces FMScope, a diagnostic framework to detect and linearly erase subject-identity confounding in frozen EEG-FM representations.

papers.title: The Identity Trap in EEG Foundation Models: A Diagnostic Audit

reasons: Explicitly targets EEG foundation models (FMs) in both title and abstract.
Proposes FMScope, a diagnostic protocol specifically for auditing frozen representations of EEG-FMs.
Evaluates multiple existing EEG-FMs, including LaBraM, CBraMod, and REVE.
Fits the inclusion criteria for EEG-FM ecosystem papers focusing on evaluation and diagnostic auditing.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a spectral audit framework that uses phase-preserving Fourier interventions to decompose physiological signals into aperiodic and periodic components. It demonstrates that model reliance on aperiodic structure is task-dependent, being dominant in sleep-wake classification and clinical abnormality detection but minimal in motor imagery. The study reveals that six of seven evaluated EEG foundation models and several ECG architectures rely significantly on these broadband features, which can be confounded by demographics like age.

headline_results: Sleep-wake classification showed high aperiodic reliance (BA drop > 0.42).
6/7 EEG foundation models showed significant aperiodic reliance on clinical EEG.
ECG models showed high aperiodic reliance (BA drop 0.32-0.36).

key_points: Benchmark framework: A spectral audit tool that quantifies model reliance on aperiodic 1/f-like envelopes across EEG and ECG tasks.
Foundation model vulnerability: Six of seven EEG foundation models showed significant aperiodic reliance on clinical EEG, suggesting scale does not eliminate broadband shortcuts.
Task-specific findings: Aperiodic reliance is high for sleep-wake and clinical abnormality detection but minimal for motor imagery.
limitations: ECG SpecParam fit quality was lower than EEG due to QRS harmonics.
PhysioNet MI trials are short, making spectral fitting more challenging.
BENDR showed intervention fragility (sham collapse), making its aperiodic reliance estimate unreliable.
The framework identifies reliance but does not provide a training-time solution to eliminate it.

method.objective: Spectral audit using phase-preserving Fourier interventions (sham, aperiodic-shaped, and flattened) to test sufficiency and necessity of spectral components.

summary.one_liner: Introduces a spectral audit framework to quantify how much EEG and ECG deep learning models rely on aperiodic 1/f-like spectral envelopes versus periodic oscillatory rhythms.
summary.title: A spectral audit framework reveals task-dependent aperiodic reliance across EEG and ECG deep learning
summary.unique_contribution: A diagnostic framework using phase-preserving Fourier interventions to disentangle aperiodic spectral reliance from oscillatory biomarkers in physiological time-series models.

papers.title: A spectral audit framework reveals task-dependent aperiodic reliance across EEG and ECG deep learning

reasons: Explicitly evaluates 'six of seven EEG foundation models' using a spectral audit framework.
Focuses on the interpretability and aperiodic reliance of representations within EEG-FMs.
Fits the ecosystem criteria for evaluation and benchmarking of foundation models.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a joint privacy audit framework for EEG foundation models (BIOT, LaBraM, EEGPT) that evaluates raw reconstruction, membership inference, identity linkage, and attribute leakage. It introduces a cross-encoder transfer audit where a ridge attribute decoder from one frozen encoder transfers to others via a linear bridge, proving that spectral attributes persist across different architectures and datasets. Empirical results across EEGMMI, Sleep-EDF, LIMO, and CHB-MIT show that standard defenses, including DP-SGD and noise injection, fail to eliminate attribute leakage without destroying task utility.

headline_results: AEDS positive (p < 0.001) across all subject-disjoint cells
Attribute leakage persists despite DP-SGD and Gaussian noise defenses

key_points: Benchmark audit of EEG foundation models: Shows that BIOT, LaBraM, and EEGPT embeddings leak spectral attributes even when they pass raw-reconstruction and membership inference tests.
Cross-encoder transferability: Demonstrates that attribute decoders trained on one model can be bridged to others, indicating a shared vulnerability across different EEG-FM architectures.
Defense failure: Finds that DP-SGD and Gaussian noise cannot close the attribute leakage channel while maintaining downstream task utility.
limitations: Does not establish population-level cross-dataset generality.
Evidence is limited to specific datasets and lacks larger clinical corpora like TUH.
Training-noise grid is a proxy and lacks a formal DP accountant.
The event-level task head used in some probes is relatively weak.
Subject-disjoint gains show transport within finite splits, not a universal law.

method.architecture: Audit framework using ridge, MLP, and kNN decoders to extract PSD attributes; employs a linear bridge for cross-encoder transfer.
method.finetuning: Linear bridge and ridge regression for attribute transfer probes.
method.objective: Audit-Endpoint Disagreement Score (AEDS) to quantify privacy leakage across multiple endpoints.
method.pretraining: Audits existing pretrained models (BIOT, LaBraM, EEGPT).

summary.one_liner: An audit framework demonstrating that EEG foundation model embeddings leak spectral attributes even when standard privacy endpoints like raw reconstruction and membership inference are cleared.
summary.title: Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a cross-encoder transfer audit and the Audit-Endpoint Disagreement Score (AEDS) to reveal spectral attribute leakage that single-endpoint privacy audits typically miss.

papers.title: Pretrained, Frozen, Still Leaking: Auditing Cross-Encoder Attribute Transfer in EEG Foundation Models

reasons: Explicitly audits and evaluates existing EEG foundation models including BIOT, LaBraM, and EEGPT.
Focuses on the security and privacy properties of pretrained EEG representations.
Fits the inclusion criteria for EEG-FM ecosystem papers regarding evaluation and auditing.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes Hypnos, a foundation model trained on over 20,000 overnight polysomnography recordings across eight sensing modalities. Unlike prior work relying on masked reconstruction or contrastive objectives, Hypnos employs an auto-regressive RQ-Transformer to predict the next discrete token. Evaluation across sleep staging and cardiac benchmarks shows it significantly outperforms existing foundation models and matches supervised baselines using only 1% of the labeled data.

headline_results: Matches U-Sleep performance using 1% of labeled data.
Outperforms OSF, SleepFM, and sleep2vec on sleep staging.
Surpasses dedicated ECG foundation models on cardiac benchmarks.

key_points: New EEG foundation model: Hypnos uses an auto-regressive RQ-Transformer to learn representations from multi-modal sleep data via next-token prediction.
Demonstrates high sample efficiency, matching supervised sleep staging performance on held-out sets while using 100x less labeled data.
Shows strong cross-domain generalization, surpassing a dedicated ECG foundation model on atrial fibrillation detection tasks.
limitations: Does not yet generalize to arbitrary high-density EEG electrode configurations.
Context length is limited and does not yet scale to hours or days of data.
Limited evaluation on broad clinical outcomes compared to some prior work.

method.architecture: RQ-Transformer (Temporal and Depth Transformers) with RVQ-based tokenization using SeaNet.
method.finetuning: Linear probing and fine-tuning on downstream sleep and cardiac tasks.
method.objective: Next-token prediction (conditional log-likelihood over next residual tokens).
method.pretraining: Self-supervised training on 20,000+ PSG recordings using CRP-based group masking for modality robustness.

summary.one_liner: Hypnos is a multi-modal sleep foundation model that utilizes next-token prediction on RVQ-tokenized physiological signals to learn generalisable representations.
summary.title: Next-Token Prediction Learns Generalisable Representations of Sleep Physiology
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces next-token prediction as a scalable self-supervised objective for multi-modal physiological signals, unifying generative modeling and representation learning.

papers.title: Next-Token Prediction Learns Generalisable Representations of Sleep Physiology

reasons: Explicitly describes 'Hypnos' as a multi-modal sleep foundation model.
Includes EEG as one of the core sensing modalities used for pretraining.
Focuses on learning generalisable representations for various downstream tasks.
Compares performance against other existing foundation models.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes a framework for generalizable EEG decoding using the BENDR Transformer-based foundation model to predict reaction times in a zero-shot cross-subject setting. To adapt the model for regression without catastrophic forgetting, the authors introduce a three-phase progressive unfreezing strategy that sequentially trains the regression head, the transformer contextualizer, and finally the full model. Using the Healthy Brain Network dataset, the approach achieved an nRMSE of 0.9799, successfully outperforming CNN baselines that failed to beat a mean-regressor baseline.

headline_results: BENDR achieved 0.9799 nRMSE, outperforming EEGNeX (0.9991 nRMSE)
tasks: Zero-shot cross-subject reaction time prediction (Contrast Change Detection task)

key_points: Method for EEG foundation model adaptation: utilizes a progressive unfreezing strategy to fine-tune the BENDR model for zero-shot cross-subject behavioral prediction.
Demonstrates that Transformer-based priors can break the 'mean-barrier' (nRMSE < 1.0) in cross-subject regression, where SOTA CNNs like EEGNeX fail.
Evaluated on the Healthy Brain Network dataset with over 3,000 subjects using a strictly subject-disjoint splitting protocol.
limitations: Restricted to 5-21 year age range
Evaluated only on a single 128-channel montage
Lack of robustness testing for different hardware or lower channel counts
Not tested on non-visual behaviors such as memory, language, or motor paradigms

method.architecture: BENDR (Convolutional Encoder + Transformer Contextualizer + Regression Head)
method.finetuning: Three-phase progressive unfreezing (Head -> Contextualizer -> Full Model) with differential learning rates
method.pretraining: Self-supervised representation learning on large-scale EEG

summary.one_liner: A zero-shot cross-subject EEG decoding framework using a fine-tuned BENDR foundation model with a progressive unfreezing strategy to predict behavioral reaction times.
summary.title: Zero-Shot Neural Priors for Generalizable Cross-Subject and Cross-Task EEG Decoding
summary.unique_contribution: A three-phase progressive unfreezing strategy with differential learning rates for adapting EEG foundation models to downstream regression tasks while preserving pretrained neural priors.

papers.title: Zero-Shot Neural Priors for Generalizable Cross-Subject and Cross-Task EEG Decoding

reasons: Explicitly benchmarks and utilizes a 'Transformer-based foundation model' for EEG decoding.
Focuses on cross-subject and cross-task generalization, which is a primary goal of EEG-FMs.
Proposes a progressive unfreezing strategy for adapting the foundation model, fitting the ecosystem criteria for adaptation and fine-tuning.

summary.detailed_summary: NeuroShield proposes a dual-stage transformer architecture to learn identity-discriminative embeddings from heterogeneous EEG recordings. It decouples temporal modeling, using ALiBi biases for length extrapolation, from spatial aggregation, which utilizes 3D coordinate embeddings for layout flexibility. Pretrained on over 15,000 subjects, the model demonstrates superior transfer performance on unseen medical and consumer-grade datasets, reducing Equal Error Rate (EER) compared to state-of-the-art fixed-input baselines.

headline_results: Reduced PEERS all-data EER from 10.25% to 7.71% using full fine-tuning
Reduced FRC-EEG EER from 14.3% to 11.2% using full fine-tuning

key_points: New EEG foundation model: NeuroShield enables device-agnostic authentication by supporting variable-channel and variable-length EEG inputs.
Dual-stage architecture: Employs a temporal transformer with ALiBi biases and a channel transformer with 3D geometry-aware embeddings to ensure spatial and temporal flexibility.
Strong transferability: Pretrained on 15,762 subjects, it reduces EER by up to 8.06 percentage points on unseen downstream datasets after fine-tuning.
limitations: Error rates are still significantly higher than established biometrics like face recognition.
Requires further expansion to larger and more diverse EEG corpora.
Needs evaluation against more sophisticated threat models beyond zero-effort impostors.
Performance on consumer-grade devices is lower than on medical-grade devices.

method.architecture: Dual-stage transformer (Temporal Transformer followed by Channel Transformer)
method.finetuning: Full-model, partial (last layers), and PEFT (adaptation modules)
method.pretraining: Pretrained on TUH-EEG, DVS-rsEEG, and PREDICT datasets

summary.one_liner: A device-agnostic foundation model for EEG authentication that handles variable channel layouts and signal durations via a dual-stage transformer.
summary.title: NeuroShield: A Device-Agnostic Foundation Model for EEG Authentication
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a dual-stage transformer architecture that decouples temporal and spatial encoding to enable EEG authentication across arbitrary channel layouts and signal durations.

papers.title: NeuroShield: A Device-Agnostic Foundation Model for EEG Authentication

reasons: Explicitly describes NeuroShield as a 'reusable foundation model for EEG authentication'.
Pretrained on a large-scale dataset comprising 15,762 subjects and 28,116 sessions.
Demonstrates transferability to unseen downstream datasets and generalization across heterogeneous recording settings.

key_points: unknown
unknown
unknown
limitations: unknown

summary.title: Evaluation of EEG Foundation Models for Event-Based Burst-Suppression Detection in ICU

papers.title: Evaluation of EEG Foundation Models for Event-Based Burst-Suppression Detection in ICU

reasons: Explicitly evaluates multiple EEG Foundation Models including REVE-base, LUNA-large, and LuMamba-Tiny
Focuses on the adaptation and fine-tuning strategies for EEG-FMs (e.g., LoRA, frozen-backbone)
Analyzes the benefit of pretrained FM representations over random initialization for a downstream clinical task

summary.detailed_summary: B[FM]^2 proposes a generative pretraining framework for EEG that operates directly on raw waveforms using optimal-transport conditional flow matching, eliminating the need for patches or tokenization. It introduces SplitUNet, which factorizes spatiotemporal convolutions into separate 1D temporal and 1D electrode operations to handle the asymmetry between dense time-series and sparse electrode arrays. The model achieves state-of-the-art results on 7 of 9 downstream tasks while requiring 30x less pretraining data than existing foundation models and generating clinically realistic synthetic EEG.

headline_results: SOTA on 7 of 9 downstream classification tasks
Suite-averaged balanced accuracy of 0.754
Synthetic EEG indistinguishable from real data by neurologists (Cohen's kappa = -0.096)

key_points: Uses continuous-time flow matching on raw waveforms instead of discrete masked-token reconstruction.
Introduces SplitUNet, which factorizes convolutions into 1D temporal and 1D electrode components to model brain signal asymmetry.
Demonstrates extreme sample efficiency, reaching SOTA on 7/9 tasks with only ~307 hours of pretraining data.
limitations: Underperforms compared to REVE on motor imagery tasks due to lack of in-domain pretraining data.
Pretraining is limited to the TUEG clinical corpus, potentially limiting generalization to non-clinical EEG.
Generative quality was validated by a small sample of only two neurologists.

method.architecture: SplitUNet (factorized 1D temporal and 1D electrode convolutions, time-only downsampling, linear-attention blocks)
method.finetuning: Linear classification head on penultimate layer activations at t=0
method.pretraining: Raw multi-channel waveform to Gaussian noise mapping

summary.one_liner: A sample-efficient EEG foundation model using continuous-time flow matching and a factorized SplitUNet architecture to avoid signal discretization.
summary.title: B[FM]$^2$: Brain Foundation Model via Flow Matching with SplitUNet
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a continuous-time flow matching paradigm for EEG pretraining combined with a factorized SplitUNet architecture that preserves electrode topology without signal discretization.

papers.title: B[FM]$^2$: Brain Foundation Model via Flow Matching with SplitUNet

reasons: Explicitly identifies as a 'Brain Foundation Model' for EEG designed for generalizable representations.
Targets single-backbone transfer across diverse clinical and BCI tasks.
Directly compares its pretraining efficiency and performance against existing EEG foundation models.

datasets: 20 datasets including EEG, iEEG, ECG, and PPG

summary.detailed_summary: SPOTR proposes a compress-reconstruct pretraining framework for EEG, iEEG, ECG, and PPG signals that forces the model to learn global representations by reconstructing the signal from a single-token bottleneck. It introduces an ST Compactor to reduce the token sequence length from C × N to C + N, significantly lowering computational costs compared to standard Transformer-based SSL. Evaluation across 17 downstream datasets demonstrates superior performance under linear probing and significantly lower inference latency and memory usage compared to general-purpose time-series models.

headline_results: Average AUC improvements: iEEG 21.71%, EEG 18.49%, ECG 17.86%, PPG 4.64%
78.1% lower p50 latency and 206.3% higher throughput than MOMENT

key_points: New EEG foundation model: A universal SSL framework (SPOTR) for physiological signals that employs a compress-reconstruct scheme with a single-token information bottleneck.
Method novelty: Uses an ST Compactor to reduce token sequence length from C × N to C + N, achieving 78% lower latency and 52% lower peak GPU memory than MOMENT.
Strong evidence: Outperforms strongest baselines in linear probing across iEEG, EEG, ECG, and PPG, with average AUC gains up to 21.71%.
limitations: Performance gains on PPG are more marginal compared to EEG, iEEG, and ECG.
Strong bottleneck may smooth out fine-grained signal details in reconstructions.
Evaluation primarily focuses on AUC, which may not capture all clinical nuances.

method.architecture: ST Compactor (spatial/temporal pooling) -> Latent Aggregator (Transformer encoder) -> Latent Renderer (Transformer decoder)
method.finetuning: Linear probing, few-shot classification, and full fine-tuning for downstream tasks
method.objective: Reconstruction loss (MSE) between original signal and reconstruction from a single-token bottleneck
method.pretraining: Self-supervised compress-reconstruct pretraining on 20 large-scale physiological datasets

summary.one_liner: A universal SSL framework for physiological signals using a single-token global bottleneck to prevent shortcut learning and an efficient spatiotemporal compaction module.
summary.title: SPOTR: Spatio-temporal Pooling One-Token Reconstruction for Universal Physiological Signal Self-supervised Learning
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a single-token global bottleneck for physiological signal reconstruction to suppress shortcut learning and an ST Compactor for efficient spatiotemporal token reduction.

papers.title: SPOTR: Spatio-temporal Pooling One-Token Reconstruction for Universal Physiological Signal Self-supervised Learning

reasons: Presents a universal pretraining framework (SPOTR) for physiological signals, with EEG as a core target modality.
Pretrained on 20 heterogeneous datasets across EEG, iEEG, ECG, and PPG to learn transferable representations.
Evaluated using linear probing to demonstrate broad transferability across datasets.
Explicitly compares its performance and efficiency against a general-purpose time-series foundation model.

summary.detailed_summary: The paper proposes EpiiSLM, which consists of a signal foundation model (SFM) and a language foundation model (LFM). The SFM uses unsupervised masked reconstruction on a large sEEG corpus followed by supervised fine-tuning anchored on a non-epileptic class to reduce label ambiguity. The LFM then integrates these signal-derived probabilities with MRI and SOZ priors to provide interpretable EZ predictions, achieving 0.978 contact-level PPV3 and 100% region-level accuracy on seizure-free patients.

headline_results: 0.978 contact-level PPV3 on MNI dataset
100% region-level accuracy for Engel Class Ia patients
0.857 PPV3 on external Brno dataset

key_points: New EEG foundation model: EpiiSLM uses a dual-system approach combining a signal transformer (BIOT) and a medical LLM (MedGemma) for epileptogenic zone localization.
Novel training strategy: Employs unsupervised pre-training on 112,096 minutes of sEEG and anchors supervised learning on a well-defined non-epileptic class to mitigate ground-truth ambiguity.
Strong clinical evidence: Achieved 0.978 contact-level PPV3 and 100% region-level accuracy in seizure-free patients, outperforming the SOZ-as-EZ baseline by 15.1%.
limitations: Small cohort size due to the invasive nature of sEEG.
Privacy and standardization constraints in cross-site data sharing.
Lack of formal quantitative evaluation of the LFM's medical reasoning.
Dependence on expert-identified MRI lesions and SOZ labels.

method.architecture: SFM based on BIOT transformer; LFM based on frozen MedGemma.
method.finetuning: Supervised fine-tuning anchored on a non-epileptic class; LFM integration via prompt engineering.
method.objective: SFM Phase I: unsupervised masked reconstruction with contrastive loss; Phase II: supervised fine-tuning using an ensemble of a One-Class Head (Deep SVDD) and a Binary Head anchored on non-epileptic signals.
method.pretraining: Unsupervised masked reconstruction on a large sEEG corpus.

summary.one_liner: A dual-foundation model system combining a signal transformer and a medical LLM to identify the epileptogenic zone from sEEG and clinical data.
summary.title: Foundation Models for Epileptogenic Zone Identification in Drug-Resistant Epilepsy
summary.unique_contribution: Introduces a dual-foundation model framework that anchors EZ identification on a grounded non-epileptic class and leverages a medical LLM for multimodal clinical integration.

papers.title: Foundation Models for Epileptogenic Zone Identification in Drug-Resistant Epilepsy

reasons: Explicitly describes the development of 'EpiiSLM, a dual foundation model system'
Mentions training a 'signal foundation model' on a large-scale sEEG dataset (104,990 minutes)
EEG (sEEG) is the primary modality and the core target of the signal foundation model

summary.title: EEG Benchmarking Needs a Task Specification Layer: NeuroDoc for Rulebook-Guided, Executable Benchmark Construction

papers.title: EEG Benchmarking Needs a Task Specification Layer: NeuroDoc for Rulebook-Guided, Executable Benchmark Construction

reasons: Explicitly targets the benchmarking and evaluation ecosystem for EEG foundation models.
Proposes a standardized task specification layer (NeuroDoc) to create reusable benchmark units for EEG-FMs.
Validates the proposed infrastructure by testing it across four different EEG foundation model backbones.
